chunks.text: \chapter{Glossary} \begin{glossary} \item[annex] A type of terminal server used to login to Instructional systems. ``Annex:'' is also the prompt you see at a terminal connected to an annex terminal server. See Section \ref{annex} for details. \it

server. See Section \ref{annex} for details. \item[argument] Additional information that the shell needs to perform your command. For example, the file name {\tt myfile} is the argument in the command \\ {\bf \% vi myfile}. \item[awk] The \ub{A}ho

nd \\ {\bf \% vi myfile}. \item[awk] The \ub{A}ho, \ub{W}einberger, \ub{K}ernighan report generator that processes ASCII text files and generates reports involving selective retrieval and string manipulation. \item[background process] Submitting co

pulation. \item[background process] Submitting commands for processing without interfering with the normal command line entries is called running in the ``background''. This is accomplished by adding the ampersand character (\&) to the end of the co

the ampersand character (\&) to the end of the command. \item[banner] The identifying bar at the top of any window, also called the ``title bar''. The initial window at login, for example, has the word {\bf Login}. When you open other windows throu

ord {\bf Login}. When you open other windows through the pull-down menu or by using {\bf xterm \&}, the banner reads {\bf xterm}. The banner is white on an active window and shaded on an inactive window. \item[client] A program which calls on a serv

ow. \item[client] A program which calls on a service to complete its function. \item[conditional execution] Executing a command based on a specified success or failure condition of a previous command. \item[conditional statement] A statement which

d. \item[conditional statement] A statement which is executed based on the evaluation of an expression. For example:\\ \medskip \hspace{1in}{\bf if (expression) statement} \item[console] The console is the terminal device to which error messages a

e is the terminal device to which error messages are written by the kernel in the event of serious system errors. \item[construct] Special commands that alter the flow of control of a shell script to execute commands conditionally or repeatedly. \i

execute commands conditionally or repeatedly. \item[C shell] A command language interpreter that contains a programming language with C-like syntax. See also {\bf shell}. \item[daemon] A UNIX\/\TM\ system task that runs as a background process (u

\ system task that runs as a background process (usually initiated at system boot time) to perform a system function. Common UNIX\/\TM\ daemons control spooled printer output, accept incoming Telnet requests, activate time-of-day scheduled tasks, etc

equests, activate time-of-day scheduled tasks, etc. \item[directory] A file that points to other files or directories. Directories can be nested to any depth. \item[Emacs] Popular public-domain full-screen editor. \item[ethernet] A 10-Mbs local a

l-screen editor. \item[ethernet] A 10-Mbs local area network using CSMA/CD (``carrier sense multiple access with collision detection'' protocol -- not covered in this manual). \item[executable] A shell script that has been given execute permission

ell script that has been given execute permission by the {\bf chmod} command so that it will be recognized and run by the shell as a command. \item[exporting a variable] A shell script sharing the value of one of its variables with any shell script

lue of one of its variables with any shell script or command that it invokes. Sending the value of a variable in one direction. \item[expression] A series of fundamental elements making up the UNIX\/\TM\ command statement that the compiler can use

/\TM\ command statement that the compiler can use to produce a value. Expressions have one or more operands and, usually, one or more operators. \item[FDDI] \ub{F}iber \ub{D}istributed \ub{D}ata \ub{I}nterchange. A 100-Mbs local area network based

{I}nterchange. A 100-Mbs local area network based on token-passing and running over fiber-optic cable. \item[file] A named collection of information. On UNIX\/\TM\ systems, a file is a stream of bytes; there are no predetermined, system-supported f

es; there are no predetermined, system-supported file or record structures. \item[foreground process] When commands are executing in the normal way such that the shell is waiting for them to finish before prompting for another command, they are said

efore prompting for another command, they are said to be ``foreground'' jobs or jobs running in the foreground. Foreground jobs can be stopped by entering commands or pressing control keys at the keyboard. \item[fork] The fork system call causes cre

oard. \item[fork] The fork system call causes creation of a new process. The new process is called a {\bf child process} and is an exact copy of the parent process, with the exception of the ID number. \item[gateway] Device that links two networks

er. \item[gateway] Device that links two networks using different protocols. It accepts all packets from each network addressed to the other, buffers them, converts them to the next format, and re-transmits them the other network. This is where pr

ransmits them the other network. This is where protocol conversion occurs. \item[graphical user interface] \ub{G}raphical \ub{U}ser \ub{I}nterface. (Pronounced ``gooey''.) Windowing systems, such as Motif, are graphical user interfaces. GUI allows

Motif, are graphical user interfaces. GUI allows use of icons, the mouse, etc. to interface with the operating system as opposed to a strictly command-based interface. \item[grep] The \ub{g}lobal \ub{r}egular \ub{e}xpression \ub{p}rinter. The {\bf

b{r}egular \ub{e}xpression \ub{p}rinter. The {\bf grep} command searches files for every occurrence of a specific word, phrase, or pattern. \item[HPUX] Hewlett Packard's (HP) version of the UNIX\/\TM\ operating system. \item[HP VUE] \ub{H}ewlett \u

\ operating system. \item[HP VUE] \ub{H}ewlett \ub{P}ackard \ub{V}isual {\ub{U}ser \ub{E}nvironment. This can be used on HP workstations instead of X Windows. \item[icon] A symbol on a display screen that you can point to with a device such as a m

en that you can point to with a device such as a mouse in order to select a particular operation or software application. \item[init process] The init process begins execution when the system starts up and is responsible for creating login processes

up and is responsible for creating login processes to wait for input from terminals. The init process is owned by the superuser and is controlled by the console. \item[in-line comment] A comment that is on the same line as a command. \item[interne

t is on the same line as a command. \item[internet] A vast collection of interconnected networks. The internet consists of many thousands of nodes, mostly universities and research institutions, throughout the world. \item[IRIX] Silicon Graphic In

oughout the world. \item[IRIX] Silicon Graphic Incorporated's (SGI) version of the UNIX\/\TM\ operating system. \item[job] A collection of processes that gets started when a command is issued. Several processes can be pipelined in a single command

al processes can be pipelined in a single command line. \item[Kerberos] Athena service that provides authentication. Kerberos provides workstations and services with encrypted ``tickets'' to be used when requesting service from a server. \item[ker

when requesting service from a server. \item[kernel] The core of the operating system that is responsible for all of the machine-level work. It is one of the system parts that cannot be modified by the average user. \item[label] A label consists

the average user. \item[label] A label consists of a name followed by a colon (:); used as a location marker in a shell script. \item[link] A link is a directory entry referring to a file. There may be several links pointing to a single file. The

ay be several links pointing to a single file. There are two kinds of links: hard links and soft (or symbolic) links. The {\bf ln} command makes both types. A {\bf hard link} is indistinguishable from an original directory entry, may not span file

om an original directory entry, may not span file systems, and may not refer to directories. A {\bf soft link} contains the name of the file to which it is linked, may span file systems, and may refer to directories. \item[literal string] A string o

to directories. \item[literal string] A string of characters enclosed in quotation marks so that they will be interpreted literally. \item[logical operator] Binary operators are {\bf \&\&} (logical AND) and {\bf $\mid$} (logical OR). \item[login

AND) and {\bf $\mid$} (logical OR). \item[login shell] The executable login command shell script file that reads the file {\tt .login} in your home directory and executes any commands there every time you login to the system. \item[loop] A sequenc

me you login to the system. \item[loop] A sequence of instructions that is executed repeatedly until a specified condition is satisfied. \item[lpd] \ub{L}ine \ub{P}rinter \ub{D}aemon. Runs as a UNIX\/\TM\ daemon on a print server system to accept

X\/\TM\ daemon on a print server system to accept and spool print to a departmental printer. \item[lpr] \ub{L}ine \ub{P}rint \ub{R}equestor. UNIX\/\TM\ task on a user workstation that communicates with the LPD to request print service. \item[man] A

th the LPD to request print service. \item[man] An command used to view selected portions of the online documentation. Man pages include information about UNIX\/\TM\ commands, as well as information about various local services and software packages

about various local services and software packages. \item[metacharacter] Many characters which are neither letters nor digits have special meaning to either the shell or to UNIX\/\TM. These characters must be quoted when used in commands in order to

s must be quoted when used in commands in order to use them without their special meaning. An example is {\bf `$>$'}. \item[MH-services] Mail Handler services. User software that provides an interface to the mail system. \item[mwm] The \ub{M}oti

ce to the mail system. \item[mwm] The \ub{M}otif \ub{W}indow \ub{M}anager is a graphical user interface. Motif controls the opening, closing, resizing, and other aspects of windows. \item[mouse] A hand-held ``locator'' attached to the workstatio

A hand-held ``locator'' attached to the workstation that is used to move a cursor on the terminal screen. \item[named account] A computer account with a login that resembles your name, as opposed to a class account with a login such as ``c199-xx''.

a class account with a login such as ``c199-xx''. \item[nesting] The placement of one set of instructions inside another, such as a loop within a loop. \item[news server] A UNIX\/\TM\ network service that acquires network news from another news ser

e that acquires network news from another news server system and spools it locally. Access to the news (over ethernet) is provided to clients via the UNIX\/\TM\ ``rn'' program using the NNTP transmission protocol. \item[NFS] \ub{N}etwork \ub{F}ile

sion protocol. \item[NFS] \ub{N}etwork \ub{F}ile \ub{S}ystem. A method for mounting and accessing file systems on remote devices over ethernet. Originally developed by Sun. We use NFS to mount Instructional file systems for use on other systems.

tructional file systems for use on other systems. \item[NIS] \ub{N}etwork \ub{I}nformation \ub{S}ystem. Sometimes called ``yellow pages''. A network lookup service consisting of databases and processes. \item[NNTP] \ub{N}etwork \ub{N}ews \ub{T}ra

sses. \item[NNTP] \ub{N}etwork \ub{N}ews \ub{T}ransport \ub{P}rotocol. Protocol used to exchange news items between two Usenet news servers (for news ``feeds") or a group of news clients and a news server (for reading news from a central source). \

erver (for reading news from a central source). \item[NTP] \ub{N}etwork \ub{T}ime \ub{P}rotocol. Protocol used to enable a UNIX\/\TM\ system to communicate via the Internet with a ``time standard'' server provided by the National Institute of Stand

server provided by the National Institute of Standards and Technology. \item[pathname] A file name. The pathname tells the shell where in the directory tree to look for the file. Files may be referenced by {\bf full pathnames} or {\bf relative pat

enced by {\bf full pathnames} or {\bf relative pathnames}. A full pathname represents the path from the root, or top, of the directory tree. A relative pathname assumes a current working directory. \item[pipe] An interprocess data channel. \item[

\item[pipe] An interprocess data channel. \item[pipeline] A sequence of one or more shell commands separated by a pipe ($|$) symbol. The standard output of each command is connected to the standard input of the next command. Each command is run as

input of the next command. Each command is run as a separate process; the shell waits for the last command to terminate. \item[process] An instance of a running program (or command) is called a process. UNIX\/\TM\ assigns each process a unique numb

ess. UNIX\/\TM\ assigns each process a unique number when it is started which is called a process identification number (PID). The PID number can be used to terminate individual processes. A user identification number (UID) is also attached to every

ntification number (UID) is also attached to every process by the operating system. When you enter any command in UNIX\/\TM\, that command is executed as a subordinate process, or {\bf child process} of a {\bf parent process}. Every process on the s

of a {\bf parent process}. Every process on the system has a parent except {\bf init}. All the processes that a user creates will be children of the login shell. \item[RISC] \ub{R}educed \ub{I}nstruction \ub{S}et \ub{C}omputer. \item[rn] \ub{R}ea

uction \ub{S}et \ub{C}omputer. \item[rn] \ub{R}ead \ub{N}ews. A UNIX\/\TM\ program that functions as the user interface for reading NNTP-delivered news from a news server. \item[root] The top level directory in the UNIX\/\TM\ hierarchical file sys

directory in the UNIX\/\TM\ hierarchical file system; is identified as the {\bf /} or ``root'' directory. The superuser of a system is also commonly called ``root''. \item[script] A file of shell commands. \item[SCSI] \ub{S}mall \ub{C}omputer \ub

ommands. \item[SCSI] \ub{S}mall \ub{C}omputer \ub{S}ystem \ub{I}nterface. (Pronounced ``scuzzy'' in the U.S. and ``sexy'' in Europe.) A standard peripheral connection bus used to connect one or more devices to a microcomputer or workstation. Instru

devices to a microcomputer or workstation. Instructional systems primarily use SCSI connections to attach disk and tape devices. SCSI devices chain to each other easily to simplify device attachment. \item[server] A machine supporting services pro

. \item[server] A machine supporting services provided to client machines. \item[service] A program running on a server which delivers some utilitarian function to one or more client systems. \item[shell] A program that runs continually, reading a

[shell] A program that runs continually, reading and interpreting key strokes. It provides the command interface between you and the system. \item[shell script] Also known as a shell program or shell procedure, it is a file containing ordinary UNIX\

procedure, it is a file containing ordinary UNIX\/\TM\ commands. Files having a {\bf \#} characters as the first character are interpreted as C shell scripts. A shell script is the UNIX\/\TM\ equivalent of a a batch file in MS-DOS, a DCL command pr

lent of a a batch file in MS-DOS, a DCL command procedure in VMS, or an execute file in WYLBUR. \item[shell variable] A named storage location that contains a value. A value is assigned to a variable by using the {\bf set} command. You can return a

e by using the {\bf set} command. You can return a variable value. For example, enter {\bf echo \$status} to display the contents of the variable {\bf status}. \item[statement] A source program or shell script is composed of a sequence of statements

ell script is composed of a sequence of statements. For example, an assignment statement is:\\ \smallskip \hspace{1in}{\bf set variable$=$value} \item[structure] A control structure may consist of nested {\bf if} conditional statements, or an {\bf i

sted {\bf if} conditional statements, or an {\bf if/then/else} conditional statement. \item[SunOS] Sun Microsystem's version of the UNIX\/\TM\ operating system. \item[superuser] The work group administrator of an UNIX\/\TM\ operating system is know

istrator of an UNIX\/\TM\ operating system is known as the superuser. The superuser (or ``root'') has special privileges, including the right to boot (start up) the system and to stop it, and set up user accounts and special user groups. \item[Sybas

ser accounts and special user groups. \item[Sybase] Commercial database system that runs under UNIX\/\TM\ . \item[title bar] See ``banner''. \item[twm] {\bf T}om's {\bf W}indow {\bf M}anager. A graphical user interface. Twm controls the opening,

aphical user interface. Twm controls the opening, closing, resizing, and other aspects of windows. \item[ULTRIX] Digital Equipment Corporation's (DEC) version of the UNIX\/\TM\ operating system. \item[utility programs] Programs included as part of

tem[utility programs] Programs included as part of the operating system such as communications software, editors, text-formatting commands, file manipulation commands, a mail utility, special program development tools, and much more. \item[vi] A ful

development tools, and much more. \item[vi] A full-screen editor developed at Berkeley. \item[window manager] A program that controls opening, closing, resizing, placement and other aspects of windows. \item[X11 (release 5)] A network protocol and

ws. \item[X11 (release 5)] A network protocol and subroutine library used to create graphics images. Windowing systems and graphics-based systems are built with X11. \item[xdm] \ub{X}11 \ub{D}isplay \ub{M}anager. This contains the initial startup

y \ub{M}anager. This contains the initial startup of X11 each time a user logs in to an X11 workstation port. \item[xmh] Graphical user interface to the MH mail-handler system. This user interface is much easier to operate than the command-based in

s much easier to operate than the command-based interface, although it can only be run from a workstation or X-terminal. \item[xterm] The X Window System terminal emulator. Also, the command used to start up an ``xterm'' on your display. } \end{glo
start up an ``xterm'' on your display. } \end{glossary}
